title: HardInterventionsCommute
tex_block_html: Let $G:=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $W_1, W_2 \ins J \cup V$ two subsets of nodes from $G$. Then we have: \[ \lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)} = \lp G_{\doit(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)} = G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}. \]

tex_statement.raw:
% Claim statement file for row claim_3_4 -- "HardInterventionsCommute".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solve_chapter.py at row start. The
% manager agent (or a worker it dispatches) fills the body below with the
% claim's statement(s) from the lecture notes -- statement only, no proof
% (proofs live in the sibling `claim_3_4_proof_*.tex` file).
%
% This file is a sibling of `main.tex` inside `<subsection>/tex/`. The
% `subfiles` package lets it render both standalone and as part of
% `main.tex`. Note: `main.tex` does NOT \subfile this statement file -- the
% sibling `_proof_` file restates the statement above the proof, so
% including both in `main.tex` would be redundant. This file exists for
% standalone reading / grep convenience.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_4 (statement)
% title: HardInterventionsCommute
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_4}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_4}

\begin{Lem}[Hard interventions commute]\label{hard-interventions-commute}
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}); throughout we use the notation of def \ref{not-cdmg} and recall the typing and axioms of def \ref{def-cdmg}, namely $J \cap V = \emptyset$, $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$, $L \subseteq V \times V$, $L$ irreflexive ($(v_1, v_2) \in L \Rightarrow v_1 \neq v_2$), and $L$ symmetric ($(v_1, v_2) \in L \Leftrightarrow (v_2, v_1) \in L$). Let
    \[ W_1 \subseteq J \cup V \quad\text{and}\quad W_2 \subseteq J \cup V \]
    be two subsets of nodes (no disjointness between $W_1$ and $W_2$ is assumed, and overlap with $J$ is permitted, as per def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention}'s precondition). All three quantifiers ``for every CDMG $G$'', ``for every $W_1 \subseteq J \cup V$'', and ``for every $W_2 \subseteq J \cup V$'' are read \emph{universally}.

    \emph{Carrier matching for the iterated hard interventions.} The inner two terms of the equality below are iterated hard interventions of the form $(G_{\doit(W_i)})_{\doit(W_j)}$, in which $\doit(W_j)$ is applied to the CDMG $G_{\doit(W_i)}$ produced by def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention}. For this outer application to be well-typed per def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention}'s precondition ``$W \subseteq J \cup V$ of the input CDMG'', we need $W_j$ to be a subset of the carrier of $G_{\doit(W_i)}$, i.e.\
    \[ W_j \;\subseteq\; J_{\doit(W_i)} \cup V_{\doit(W_i)}. \]
    This holds because hard intervention preserves the carrier $J \cup V$ as a set: by items i.\ and ii.\ of def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention},
    \[
        J_{\doit(W_i)} \cup V_{\doit(W_i)} \;=\; (J \cup W_i) \cup (V \sm W_i) \;=\; J \cup V,
    \]
    where the second equality uses $W_i \subseteq J \cup V$ (as in the carrier-matching paragraph of \ref{claim_3_3}). Hence $W_j \subseteq J \cup V = J_{\doit(W_i)} \cup V_{\doit(W_i)}$, so $(G_{\doit(W_i)})_{\doit(W_j)}$ is defined. The same observation applied to $W_1 \cup W_2 \subseteq J \cup V$ shows that the right-hand side $G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}$ is also defined per def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention}.

    \emph{The conclusion.} The CDMGs $(G_{\doit(W_1)})_{\doit(W_2)}$, $(G_{\doit(W_2)})_{\doit(W_1)}$, and $G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}$ (all constructed via def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention} as just verified) coincide. Concretely, the LN's displayed triple equality
    \[ \lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)} = \lp G_{\doit(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)} = G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)} \]
    is the conjunction of the two following equalities of CDMGs:
    \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
        \item \emph{Composition with $W_1$ then $W_2$.}
              \[ \lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)} \;=\; G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}. \]
        \item \emph{Composition with $W_2$ then $W_1$.}
              \[ \lp G_{\doit(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)} \;=\; G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}. \]
    \end{enumerate}
    Together (a) and (b) yield the LN's ``symmetry under swap'' reading $\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)} = \lp G_{\doit(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}$ by transitivity of equality. The equality of CDMGs in (a) and (b) is understood componentwise on the four components $(J, V, E, L)$ of def \ref{def-cdmg}, but the conjunctive unpacking into the four field-by-field equalities is deferred to the proof.

      %This shows that our notion:
      %\[ G(V \sm (W_1 \cup W_2) | \doit(W_1 \cup W_2 \cup J))   \]
      %is unambiguous.
\end{Lem}

\end{document}
tex_statement.html:
Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg); throughout we use the notation of def not-cdmg and recall the typing and axioms of def def-cdmg, namely $J \cap V = \emptyset$, $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$, $L \subseteq V \times V$, $L$ irreflexive ($(v_1, v_2) \in L \Rightarrow v_1 \neq v_2$), and $L$ symmetric ($(v_1, v_2) \in L \Leftrightarrow (v_2, v_1) \in L$). Let \[ W_1 \subseteq J \cup V \quad\text{and}\quad W_2 \subseteq J \cup V \] be two subsets of nodes (no disjointness between $W_1$ and $W_2$ is assumed, and overlap with $J$ is permitted, as per def def:G_hard_intervention's precondition). All three quantifiers “for every CDMG $G$”, “for every $W_1 \subseteq J \cup V$”, and “for every $W_2 \subseteq J \cup V$” are read universally.

Carrier matching for the iterated hard interventions. The inner two terms of the equality below are iterated hard interventions of the form $(G_{\doit(W_i)})_{\doit(W_j)}$, in which $\doit(W_j)$ is applied to the CDMG $G_{\doit(W_i)}$ produced by def def:G_hard_intervention. For this outer application to be well-typed per def def:G_hard_intervention's precondition “$W \subseteq J \cup V$ of the input CDMG”, we need $W_j$ to be a subset of the carrier of $G_{\doit(W_i)}$, i.e.\ \[ W_j \;\subseteq\; J_{\doit(W_i)} \cup V_{\doit(W_i)}. \] This holds because hard intervention preserves the carrier $J \cup V$ as a set: by items i.\ and ii.\ of def def:G_hard_intervention, \[ J_{\doit(W_i)} \cup V_{\doit(W_i)} \;=\; (J \cup W_i) \cup (V \sm W_i) \;=\; J \cup V, \] where the second equality uses $W_i \subseteq J \cup V$ (as in the carrier-matching paragraph of claim_3_3). Hence $W_j \subseteq J \cup V = J_{\doit(W_i)} \cup V_{\doit(W_i)}$, so $(G_{\doit(W_i)})_{\doit(W_j)}$ is defined. The same observation applied to $W_1 \cup W_2 \subseteq J \cup V$ shows that the right-hand side $G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}$ is also defined per def def:G_hard_intervention.

The conclusion. The CDMGs $(G_{\doit(W_1)})_{\doit(W_2)}$, $(G_{\doit(W_2)})_{\doit(W_1)}$, and $G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}$ (all constructed via def def:G_hard_intervention as just verified) coincide. Concretely, the LN's displayed triple equality \[ \lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)} = \lp G_{\doit(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)} = G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)} \] is the conjunction of the two following equalities of CDMGs:

  1. Composition with $W_1$ then $W_2$. \[ \lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)} \;=\; G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}. \]
  2. Composition with $W_2$ then $W_1$. \[ \lp G_{\doit(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)} \;=\; G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}. \]

Together (a) and (b) yield the LN's “symmetry under swap” reading $\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)} = \lp G_{\doit(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}$ by transitivity of equality. The equality of CDMGs in (a) and (b) is understood componentwise on the four components $(J, V, E, L)$ of def def-cdmg, but the conjunctive unpacking into the four field-by-field equalities is deferred to the proof.

% Claim proof file for row claim_3_4 -- "HardInterventionsCommute".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solve_chapter.py at row start. A manager
% agent dedicated to writing the TeX proof fills in the proof body below.
%
% This file is a sibling of `main.tex` inside `<subsection>/tex/`. The
% `subfiles` package lets it render both standalone and as part of
% `main.tex` via `\subfile{...}`.
%
% The statement is restated above the proof so the file is self-contained
% when rendered on its own. The proof must:
%   - Stay close to the lecture notes' proof if one exists (always search
%     the LN first for a `\begin{proof}` block immediately following the
%     claim; copy / lightly edit when present).
%   - Otherwise use the LN paradigm and cite prior claims by their `ref`.
%   - Be self-contained enough that a mathematician can follow it without
%     re-reading the LN.
%   - Have no `sorry`, `omitted`, or "obvious" shortcuts.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: claim_3_4 (proof, with statement restated for readability)
% title: HardInterventionsCommute
\def\rowref{claim\_3\_4}
\phantomsection\label{claim_3_4}

% --- statement (restated) ---------------------------------------------------
\begin{Lem}[Hard interventions commute]\label{hard-interventions-commute}
    Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def \ref{def-cdmg}); throughout we use the notation of def \ref{not-cdmg} and recall the typing and axioms of def \ref{def-cdmg}, namely $J \cap V = \emptyset$, $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$, $L \subseteq V \times V$, $L$ irreflexive ($(v_1, v_2) \in L \Rightarrow v_1 \neq v_2$), and $L$ symmetric ($(v_1, v_2) \in L \Leftrightarrow (v_2, v_1) \in L$). Let
    \[ W_1 \subseteq J \cup V \quad\text{and}\quad W_2 \subseteq J \cup V \]
    be two subsets of nodes (no disjointness between $W_1$ and $W_2$ is assumed, and overlap with $J$ is permitted, as per def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention}'s precondition). All three quantifiers ``for every CDMG $G$'', ``for every $W_1 \subseteq J \cup V$'', and ``for every $W_2 \subseteq J \cup V$'' are read \emph{universally}.

    \emph{Carrier matching for the iterated hard interventions.} The inner two terms of the equality below are iterated hard interventions of the form $(G_{\doit(W_i)})_{\doit(W_j)}$, in which $\doit(W_j)$ is applied to the CDMG $G_{\doit(W_i)}$ produced by def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention}. For this outer application to be well-typed per def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention}'s precondition ``$W \subseteq J \cup V$ of the input CDMG'', we need $W_j$ to be a subset of the carrier of $G_{\doit(W_i)}$, i.e.\
    \[ W_j \;\subseteq\; J_{\doit(W_i)} \cup V_{\doit(W_i)}. \]
    This holds because hard intervention preserves the carrier $J \cup V$ as a set: by items i.\ and ii.\ of def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention},
    \[
        J_{\doit(W_i)} \cup V_{\doit(W_i)} \;=\; (J \cup W_i) \cup (V \sm W_i) \;=\; J \cup V,
    \]
    where the second equality uses $W_i \subseteq J \cup V$ (as in the carrier-matching paragraph of \ref{claim_3_3}). Hence $W_j \subseteq J \cup V = J_{\doit(W_i)} \cup V_{\doit(W_i)}$, so $(G_{\doit(W_i)})_{\doit(W_j)}$ is defined. The same observation applied to $W_1 \cup W_2 \subseteq J \cup V$ shows that the right-hand side $G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}$ is also defined per def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention}.

    \emph{The conclusion.} The CDMGs $(G_{\doit(W_1)})_{\doit(W_2)}$, $(G_{\doit(W_2)})_{\doit(W_1)}$, and $G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}$ (all constructed via def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention} as just verified) coincide. Concretely, the LN's displayed triple equality
    \[ \lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)} = \lp G_{\doit(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)} = G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)} \]
    is the conjunction of the two following equalities of CDMGs:
    \begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
        \item \emph{Composition with $W_1$ then $W_2$.}
              \[ \lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)} \;=\; G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}. \]
        \item \emph{Composition with $W_2$ then $W_1$.}
              \[ \lp G_{\doit(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)} \;=\; G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}. \]
    \end{enumerate}
    Together (a) and (b) yield the LN's ``symmetry under swap'' reading $\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)} = \lp G_{\doit(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}$ by transitivity of equality. The equality of CDMGs in (a) and (b) is understood componentwise on the four components $(J, V, E, L)$ of def \ref{def-cdmg}, but the conjunctive unpacking into the four field-by-field equalities is deferred to the proof.

      %This shows that our notion:
      %\[ G(V \sm (W_1 \cup W_2) | \doit(W_1 \cup W_2 \cup J))   \]
      %is unambiguous.
\end{Lem}

% --- proof ------------------------------------------------------------------
% Proof body adapted from the LN's \Claude{...} block at
% lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex lines 326-355, with three light edits:
% (i) the opening is re-framed to reference the rewrite's (a) and (b) labels,
% (ii) the closing "by symmetry" remark for (b) is expanded into an explicit
% W_1 <-> W_2 swap + commutativity-of-union step, and
% (iii) the bidirected-edge paragraph carries an inline note flagging the
% Lean-side two-sided filter on L (registered deviation
% hard_intervention_l_symmetrized_removal attached to def_3_10's encoding).
\begin{proof}
We prove sub-claim (a), $\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)} = G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}$, by checking the equality of the two CDMGs componentwise on each of the four components $(J, V, E, L)$ of def \ref{def-cdmg} in turn; sub-claim (b), $\lp G_{\doit(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)} = G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}$, is then obtained by an explicit symmetry argument at the end of the proof. Both sub-claims rely on the carrier-matching observation already established in the statement block above to license the outer $\doit(\cdot)$ application.

\medskip\noindent\textbf{Proof of (a): composition with $W_1$ then $W_2$.}
We verify the equality $\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)} = G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}$ on each of the four components.

\medskip\noindent\emph{Input nodes.}
By item i.\ of def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention} applied twice (first to $G$ with $W_1$, then to $G_{\doit(W_1)}$ with $W_2$),
\[
    J_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)}} \;=\; J_{\doit(W_1)} \cup W_2 \;=\; (J \cup W_1) \cup W_2 \;=\; J \cup (W_1 \cup W_2) \;=\; J_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)},
\]
where the middle equality is associativity of $\cup$. \checkmark

\medskip\noindent\emph{Output nodes.}
By item ii.\ of def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention} applied twice,
\[
    V_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)}} \;=\; V_{\doit(W_1)} \sm W_2 \;=\; (V \sm W_1) \sm W_2 \;=\; V \sm (W_1 \cup W_2) \;=\; V_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)},
\]
where the middle equality is the standard identity $(A \sm B) \sm C = A \sm (B \cup C)$ for set difference (an element $x$ survives both removals iff $x \notin B$ and $x \notin C$, iff $x \notin B \cup C$). \checkmark

\medskip\noindent\emph{Directed edges.}
By item iii.\ of def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention} applied twice,
\begin{align*}
    E_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)}}
    &= E_{\doit(W_1)} \sm \lC v \tuh w \mid w \in W_2 \rC \\
    &= \lp E \sm \lC v \tuh w \mid w \in W_1 \rC \rp
       \sm \lC v \tuh w \mid w \in W_2 \rC \\
    &= E \sm \lC v \tuh w \mid w \in W_1 \cup W_2 \rC
     = E_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}.
\end{align*}
The penultimate equality is the same set-difference identity used for the output nodes, instantiated at the directed-edge set: an ordered pair $(v, w) \in E$ survives both removal steps iff its head $w$ lies neither in $W_1$ nor in $W_2$, iff $w \notin W_1 \cup W_2$; that condition is exactly the one matching the removal set of a single application of item iii.\ at the union $W_1 \cup W_2$. \checkmark

\medskip\noindent\emph{Bidirected edges.}
By item iv.\ of def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention} applied twice,
\begin{align*}
    L_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)}}
    &= L_{\doit(W_1)} \sm \lC v \huh w \mid w \in W_2 \rC \\
    &= \lp L \sm \lC v \huh w \mid w \in W_1 \rC \rp
       \sm \lC v \huh w \mid w \in W_2 \rC \\
    &= L \sm \lC v \huh w \mid w \in W_1 \cup W_2 \rC
     = L_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}.
\end{align*}
The penultimate equality combines the two removal clauses by the same union-of-conditions step used for the directed-edge case, with $v \huh w$ in place of $v \tuh w$. \checkmark

\smallskip\noindent\emph{Remark on the Lean encoding of $L$.} In the Lean formalisation of def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention}, item iv.\ is implemented with a \emph{two-sided} filter on the bidirected-edge set: the ordered pair $(v_1, v_2) \in L$ is retained iff $v_1 \notin W$ \emph{and} $v_2 \notin W$, rather than the LN's literal one-sided ``$v_2 \notin W$'' clause. This two-sided convention is the symmetrisation forced by the CDMG axioms (it is the registered deviation \emph{hard\_intervention\_l\_symmetrized\_removal}, attached to def\_3\_10's encoding and explained in the remark of def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention}: the literal one-sided formula does not preserve the symmetry of $L$). The deviation is harmless for the composition step above because $L$ is $\Sym$-symmetric and because composition of two such filters reads identically in either convention: ``filter-by-$p$ then filter-by-$q$ = filter-by-$(p \wedge q)$'' applied with $p(v_1, v_2) := (v_1 \notin W_1) \wedge (v_2 \notin W_1)$ and $q(v_1, v_2) := (v_1 \notin W_2) \wedge (v_2 \notin W_2)$ yields the predicate $(v_1, v_2) \mapsto (v_1 \notin W_1 \cup W_2) \wedge (v_2 \notin W_1 \cup W_2)$, which is precisely the two-sided filter for the union $W_1 \cup W_2$. Both the LN one-sided convention and the Lean two-sided convention therefore land on the same final bidirected-edge set $L_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}$.

\smallskip
Combining the four componentwise equalities above, the CDMGs $\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)}$ and $G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}$ agree field by field on each of $(J, V, E, L)$ and are therefore equal as CDMGs (def \ref{def-cdmg}). This establishes sub-claim (a).

\medskip\noindent\textbf{Proof of (b): composition with $W_2$ then $W_1$.}
Sub-claim (b) is obtained by swapping the roles of $W_1$ and $W_2$ in the argument for (a). Concretely, re-running the four-component check above with the substitution $W_1 \leftrightarrow W_2$ -- the proof depends on the pair of subsets only through their symbolic roles, and the displayed identities $(J \cup W_1) \cup W_2 = J \cup (W_1 \cup W_2)$, $(V \sm W_1) \sm W_2 = V \sm (W_1 \cup W_2)$, and the analogous edge-set identities all retain their truth under the swap -- yields
\[
    \lp G_{\doit(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)} \;=\; G_{\doit(W_2 \cup W_1)}.
\]
This is precisely sub-claim (a) instantiated at the swapped pair $(W_2, W_1)$. We emphasise that sub-claim (b) is \emph{not} a literal instance of (a): the iterated hard intervention on the left-hand side of (b) is built from the swapped pair $(W_2, W_1)$, and the right-hand-side union appearing in (a) for that swapped pair is $W_2 \cup W_1$, not the $W_1 \cup W_2$ in the statement of (b). The two right-hand sides agree by commutativity of union, $W_2 \cup W_1 = W_1 \cup W_2$, which gives
\[
    G_{\doit(W_2 \cup W_1)} \;=\; G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}
\]
as CDMGs: def \ref{def:G_hard_intervention} depends on $W$ only through the underlying set $W \subseteq J \cup V$ (each of items i.\ -- iv.\ refers to $W$ only via the operations $J \cup W$, $V \sm W$, and the membership predicate ``$\cdot \in W$''), and equal sets produce equal CDMGs componentwise. Chaining the two equalities,
\[
    \lp G_{\doit(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)} \;=\; G_{\doit(W_2 \cup W_1)} \;=\; G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)},
\]
establishes sub-claim (b).

\medskip\noindent\emph{Recovery of the LN's triple equality.}
By sub-claims (a) and (b) -- both of which equate their respective iterated hard intervention with the single right-hand side $G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}$ -- transitivity of equality on that shared right-hand side yields
\[
    \lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)} \;=\; G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)} \;=\; \lp G_{\doit(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)},
\]
which is the LN's stated triple equality and in particular recovers the swap-symmetry reading $\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)} = \lp G_{\doit(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}$.
\end{proof}

\end{document}
tex_proof.html:
Lemma — Hard interventions commute

Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg); throughout we use the notation of def not-cdmg and recall the typing and axioms of def def-cdmg, namely $J \cap V = \emptyset$, $E \subseteq (J \cup V) \times V$, $L \subseteq V \times V$, $L$ irreflexive ($(v_1, v_2) \in L \Rightarrow v_1 \neq v_2$), and $L$ symmetric ($(v_1, v_2) \in L \Leftrightarrow (v_2, v_1) \in L$). Let \[ W_1 \subseteq J \cup V \quad\text{and}\quad W_2 \subseteq J \cup V \] be two subsets of nodes (no disjointness between $W_1$ and $W_2$ is assumed, and overlap with $J$ is permitted, as per def def:G_hard_intervention's precondition). All three quantifiers “for every CDMG $G$”, “for every $W_1 \subseteq J \cup V$”, and “for every $W_2 \subseteq J \cup V$” are read universally.

Carrier matching for the iterated hard interventions. The inner two terms of the equality below are iterated hard interventions of the form $(G_{\doit(W_i)})_{\doit(W_j)}$, in which $\doit(W_j)$ is applied to the CDMG $G_{\doit(W_i)}$ produced by def def:G_hard_intervention. For this outer application to be well-typed per def def:G_hard_intervention's precondition “$W \subseteq J \cup V$ of the input CDMG”, we need $W_j$ to be a subset of the carrier of $G_{\doit(W_i)}$, i.e.\ \[ W_j \;\subseteq\; J_{\doit(W_i)} \cup V_{\doit(W_i)}. \] This holds because hard intervention preserves the carrier $J \cup V$ as a set: by items i.\ and ii.\ of def def:G_hard_intervention, \[ J_{\doit(W_i)} \cup V_{\doit(W_i)} \;=\; (J \cup W_i) \cup (V \sm W_i) \;=\; J \cup V, \] where the second equality uses $W_i \subseteq J \cup V$ (as in the carrier-matching paragraph of claim_3_3). Hence $W_j \subseteq J \cup V = J_{\doit(W_i)} \cup V_{\doit(W_i)}$, so $(G_{\doit(W_i)})_{\doit(W_j)}$ is defined. The same observation applied to $W_1 \cup W_2 \subseteq J \cup V$ shows that the right-hand side $G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}$ is also defined per def def:G_hard_intervention.

The conclusion. The CDMGs $(G_{\doit(W_1)})_{\doit(W_2)}$, $(G_{\doit(W_2)})_{\doit(W_1)}$, and $G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}$ (all constructed via def def:G_hard_intervention as just verified) coincide. Concretely, the LN's displayed triple equality \[ \lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)} = \lp G_{\doit(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)} = G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)} \] is the conjunction of the two following equalities of CDMGs:

  1. Composition with $W_1$ then $W_2$. \[ \lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)} \;=\; G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}. \]
  2. Composition with $W_2$ then $W_1$. \[ \lp G_{\doit(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)} \;=\; G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}. \]

Together (a) and (b) yield the LN's “symmetry under swap” reading $\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)} = \lp G_{\doit(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}$ by transitivity of equality. The equality of CDMGs in (a) and (b) is understood componentwise on the four components $(J, V, E, L)$ of def def-cdmg, but the conjunctive unpacking into the four field-by-field equalities is deferred to the proof.

Proof.

We prove sub-claim (a), $\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)} = G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}$, by checking the equality of the two CDMGs componentwise on each of the four components $(J, V, E, L)$ of def def-cdmg in turn; sub-claim (b), $\lp G_{\doit(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)} = G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}$, is then obtained by an explicit symmetry argument at the end of the proof. Both sub-claims rely on the carrier-matching observation already established in the statement block above to license the outer $\doit(\cdot)$ application.

Proof of (a): composition with $W_1$ then $W_2$. We verify the equality $\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)} = G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}$ on each of the four components.

Input nodes. By item i.\ of def def:G_hard_intervention applied twice (first to $G$ with $W_1$, then to $G_{\doit(W_1)}$ with $W_2$), \[ J_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)}} \;=\; J_{\doit(W_1)} \cup W_2 \;=\; (J \cup W_1) \cup W_2 \;=\; J \cup (W_1 \cup W_2) \;=\; J_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}, \] where the middle equality is associativity of $\cup$. \checkmark

Output nodes. By item ii.\ of def def:G_hard_intervention applied twice, \[ V_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)}} \;=\; V_{\doit(W_1)} \sm W_2 \;=\; (V \sm W_1) \sm W_2 \;=\; V \sm (W_1 \cup W_2) \;=\; V_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}, \] where the middle equality is the standard identity $(A \sm B) \sm C = A \sm (B \cup C)$ for set difference (an element $x$ survives both removals iff $x \notin B$ and $x \notin C$, iff $x \notin B \cup C$). \checkmark

Directed edges. By item iii.\ of def def:G_hard_intervention applied twice, \[\begin{aligned} E_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)}} &= E_{\doit(W_1)} \sm \lC v \tuh w \mid w \in W_2 \rC \\ &= \lp E \sm \lC v \tuh w \mid w \in W_1 \rC \rp \sm \lC v \tuh w \mid w \in W_2 \rC \\ &= E \sm \lC v \tuh w \mid w \in W_1 \cup W_2 \rC = E_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}. \end{aligned}\] The penultimate equality is the same set-difference identity used for the output nodes, instantiated at the directed-edge set: an ordered pair $(v, w) \in E$ survives both removal steps iff its head $w$ lies neither in $W_1$ nor in $W_2$, iff $w \notin W_1 \cup W_2$; that condition is exactly the one matching the removal set of a single application of item iii.\ at the union $W_1 \cup W_2$. \checkmark

Bidirected edges. By item iv.\ of def def:G_hard_intervention applied twice, \[\begin{aligned} L_{\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)}} &= L_{\doit(W_1)} \sm \lC v \huh w \mid w \in W_2 \rC \\ &= \lp L \sm \lC v \huh w \mid w \in W_1 \rC \rp \sm \lC v \huh w \mid w \in W_2 \rC \\ &= L \sm \lC v \huh w \mid w \in W_1 \cup W_2 \rC = L_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}. \end{aligned}\] The penultimate equality combines the two removal clauses by the same union-of-conditions step used for the directed-edge case, with $v \huh w$ in place of $v \tuh w$. \checkmark

Remark on the Lean encoding of $L$. In the Lean formalisation of def def:G_hard_intervention, item iv.\ is implemented with a two-sided filter on the bidirected-edge set: the ordered pair $(v_1, v_2) \in L$ is retained iff $v_1 \notin W$ and $v_2 \notin W$, rather than the LN's literal one-sided “$v_2 \notin W$” clause. This two-sided convention is the symmetrisation forced by the CDMG axioms (it is the registered deviation hard\_intervention\_l\_symmetrized\_removal, attached to def\_3\_10's encoding and explained in the remark of def def:G_hard_intervention: the literal one-sided formula does not preserve the symmetry of $L$). The deviation is harmless for the composition step above because $L$ is $\Sym$-symmetric and because composition of two such filters reads identically in either convention: “filter-by-$p$ then filter-by-$q$ = filter-by-$(p \wedge q)$” applied with $p(v_1, v_2) := (v_1 \notin W_1) \wedge (v_2 \notin W_1)$ and $q(v_1, v_2) := (v_1 \notin W_2) \wedge (v_2 \notin W_2)$ yields the predicate $(v_1, v_2) \mapsto (v_1 \notin W_1 \cup W_2) \wedge (v_2 \notin W_1 \cup W_2)$, which is precisely the two-sided filter for the union $W_1 \cup W_2$. Both the LN one-sided convention and the Lean two-sided convention therefore land on the same final bidirected-edge set $L_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}$.

Combining the four componentwise equalities above, the CDMGs $\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)}$ and $G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}$ agree field by field on each of $(J, V, E, L)$ and are therefore equal as CDMGs (def def-cdmg). This establishes sub-claim (a).

Proof of (b): composition with $W_2$ then $W_1$. Sub-claim (b) is obtained by swapping the roles of $W_1$ and $W_2$ in the argument for (a). Concretely, re-running the four-component check above with the substitution $W_1 \leftrightarrow W_2$ – the proof depends on the pair of subsets only through their symbolic roles, and the displayed identities $(J \cup W_1) \cup W_2 = J \cup (W_1 \cup W_2)$, $(V \sm W_1) \sm W_2 = V \sm (W_1 \cup W_2)$, and the analogous edge-set identities all retain their truth under the swap – yields \[ \lp G_{\doit(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)} \;=\; G_{\doit(W_2 \cup W_1)}. \] This is precisely sub-claim (a) instantiated at the swapped pair $(W_2, W_1)$. We emphasise that sub-claim (b) is not a literal instance of (a): the iterated hard intervention on the left-hand side of (b) is built from the swapped pair $(W_2, W_1)$, and the right-hand-side union appearing in (a) for that swapped pair is $W_2 \cup W_1$, not the $W_1 \cup W_2$ in the statement of (b). The two right-hand sides agree by commutativity of union, $W_2 \cup W_1 = W_1 \cup W_2$, which gives \[ G_{\doit(W_2 \cup W_1)} \;=\; G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)} \] as CDMGs: def def:G_hard_intervention depends on $W$ only through the underlying set $W \subseteq J \cup V$ (each of items i.\ – iv.\ refers to $W$ only via the operations $J \cup W$, $V \sm W$, and the membership predicate “$\cdot \in W$”), and equal sets produce equal CDMGs componentwise. Chaining the two equalities, \[ \lp G_{\doit(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)} \;=\; G_{\doit(W_2 \cup W_1)} \;=\; G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}, \] establishes sub-claim (b).

Recovery of the LN's triple equality. By sub-claims (a) and (b) – both of which equate their respective iterated hard intervention with the single right-hand side $G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)}$ – transitivity of equality on that shared right-hand side yields \[ \lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)} \;=\; G_{\doit(W_1 \cup W_2)} \;=\; \lp G_{\doit(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}, \] which is the LN's stated triple equality and in particular recovers the swap-symmetry reading $\lp G_{\doit(W_1)} \rp_{\doit(W_2)} = \lp G_{\doit(W_2)} \rp_{\doit(W_1)}$.

lean_blocks.explanation: This is a `variable` declaration: Lean auto-binds `Node : Type*` and the instance `[DecidableEq Node]` as implicit arguments to every subsequent declaration in the surrounding `CDMG` namespace, sparing us from rewriting the same parameters on `subset_carrier_of_hardInterventionOn` and on `hardInterventionsCommute`. The `Type*` notation makes `Node` universe-polymorphic, so callers can instantiate it at any universe level. The `DecidableEq Node` assumption is load-bearing for this row in particular: the statement references `Finset.union` (forming `G.J ∪ W` and `W₁ ∪ W₂`), `Finset.sdiff` (the `\` in `G.V \ W`), and `Finset.filter` (used inside `hardInterventionOn` and reasoned about via `Finset.filter_filter` and `Finset.filter_congr` in the proof) — all of which need decidable equality on the element type to compute the resulting `Finset`. Stronger structure on `Node` (e.g.\ `Fintype`, `LinearOrder`) is not required here and is deferred to downstream consumers that actually use it. Same convention as the rest of `Chapter3_GraphTheory`'s files.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Auto-binds `Node` + `DecidableEq Node` as implicits; decidability drives `Finset.union`/`sdiff`/`filter` used below.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.code:
private lemma subset_carrier_of_hardInterventionOn
    {G : CDMG Node} {W : Finset Node} (hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V)
    {S : Finset Node} (hS : S ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V) :
    S ⊆ (G.hardInterventionOn W hW).J ∪ (G.hardInterventionOn W hW).V
lean_blocks.explanation: This block is the load-bearing statement helper for the commutativity claim. The `private lemma` records that any subset $S \subseteq J \cup V$ of the original carrier remains a subset of the intervened carrier $J_{\doit(W)} \cup V_{\doit(W)} = (J \cup W) \cup (V \setminus W)$. That fact is what lets the main theorem state `(G.hardInterventionOn W₁ hW₁).hardInterventionOn W₂ ...` with a *single* subset hypothesis: applying `hardInterventionOn` doesn't shrink the carrier, so the same $hW_2$ that types the outer call still types the inner one. The signature is wrapped in `--- start helper` / `--- end helper` markers (with the `:= by ...` proof body sitting below the end marker per the signature-only helper-wrap convention) because the main theorem's signature cannot type-check without it — the iterated `hardInterventionOn` call needs this subset fact at the type level. Implementation: the proof unfolds the carrier of `hardInterventionOn` and routes membership through `Finset.subset_union_left` / `Finset.subset_union_right` chained with the original `hS`.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- ref: claim_3_4 (helper) — statement-supporting lemma: subsets of `J ∪ V` survive `hardInterventionOn`.
-- Used inside the main theorem's signature to type the inner-then-outer iterated call.
private lemma subset_carrier_of_hardInterventionOn
    {G : CDMG Node} {W : Finset Node} (hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V)
    {S : Finset Node} (hS : S ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V) :
    S ⊆ (G.hardInterventionOn W hW).J ∪ (G.hardInterventionOn W hW).V

lean_blocks.code:
theorem hardInterventionsCommute (G : CDMG Node) (W₁ W₂ : Finset Node)
    (hW₁ : W₁ ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V) (hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V) :
    (G.hardInterventionOn W₁ hW₁).hardInterventionOn W₂
        (subset_carrier_of_hardInterventionOn hW₁ hW₂)
      = G.hardInterventionOn (W₁ ∪ W₂) (Finset.union_subset hW₁ hW₂)
    ∧
    (G.hardInterventionOn W₂ hW₂).hardInterventionOn W₁
        (subset_carrier_of_hardInterventionOn hW₂ hW₁)
      = G.hardInterventionOn (W₁ ∪ W₂) (Finset.union_subset hW₁ hW₂)
lean_blocks.explanation: This is the row's main lemma: hard intervention commutes with itself, and an iterated intervention collapses to a single intervention on the union. The statement quantifies over a `CDMG Node`, two intervention sets `W₁ W₂ : Finset Node`, and the LN's pair of subset hypotheses `hW₁`, `hW₂`; the conclusion is the conjunction `(a) ∧ (b)` of the two binary equalities the rewritten tex splits the LN's triple equality into. Lean has no native triple-equality syntax, so the swap-symmetry reading $(G_{\doit(W_1)})_{\doit(W_2)} = (G_{\doit(W_2)})_{\doit(W_1)}$ is recovered by users as `.1.trans .2.symm`. Two Mathlib idioms power the right-hand side: `Finset.union_subset hW₁ hW₂` packages $W_1, W_2 \subseteq J \cup V \Rightarrow W_1 \cup W_2 \subseteq J \cup V$, supplying the well-typedness witness for `G.hardInterventionOn (W₁ ∪ W₂)`; the helper `subset_carrier_of_hardInterventionOn hW₁ hW₂` supplies the inner-`hW` proof term needed by the nested intervention on the left, since `W₂` must sit inside the inner-intervened carrier, not just inside `G.J ∪ G.V`. No disjointness binder is imposed on `W₁`, `W₂` — the lemma is a free composition result.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- ref: claim_3_4 (main) — pair (a) ∧ (b) of binary CDMG equalities; LN's triple equality recovered via `.1.trans .2.symm`.
theorem hardInterventionsCommute (G : CDMG Node) (W₁ W₂ : Finset Node)
    -- LN-faithful precondition: each `Wᵢ` lives inside the carrier `G.J ∪ G.V`; no disjointness hypothesis.
    (hW₁ : W₁ ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V) (hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V) :
    -- (a) Intervening with `W₁` then `W₂` equals intervening once with the union.
    (G.hardInterventionOn W₁ hW₁).hardInterventionOn W₂
        -- Inner-`hW` for the nested call: transports `hW₂` across the first intervention.
        (subset_carrier_of_hardInterventionOn hW₁ hW₂)
      -- RHS: `Finset.union_subset` packages `W₁ ∪ W₂ ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V`.
      = G.hardInterventionOn (W₁ ∪ W₂) (Finset.union_subset hW₁ hW₂)
    ∧
    -- (b) Same equality with `W₁`, `W₂` swapped on the left; RHS shared with (a).
    (G.hardInterventionOn W₂ hW₂).hardInterventionOn W₁
        -- Swapped roles in the helper call: inner is `W₂` (via `hW₂`), transported set is `W₁` (via `hW₁`).
        (subset_carrier_of_hardInterventionOn hW₂ hW₁)
      = G.hardInterventionOn (W₁ ∪ W₂) (Finset.union_subset hW₁ hW₂)

design_choices:
**One theorem returning a conjunction, not two separate top-level theorems.** The LN packages its triple equality $(G_{\mathrm{do}(W_1)})_{\mathrm{do}(W_2)} = (G_{\mathrm{do}(W_2)})_{\mathrm{do}(W_1)} = G_{\mathrm{do}(W_1 \cup W_2)}$ inside a single lemma block, and the canonical statement decomposes it into two binary equalities (a) and (b). We mirror that single-lemma packaging by returning `(a) ∧ (b)` from one theorem. Consumers reach `.1` for (a) and `.2` for (b); the LN's swap-symmetry reading $(G_{\mathrm{do}(W_1)})_{\mathrm{do}(W_2)} = (G_{\mathrm{do}(W_2)})_{\mathrm{do}(W_1)}$ is then recovered as `.1.trans .2.symm`, with no need to bake a third sub-claim into the statement. Splitting into two top-level theorems was rejected because it would duplicate the antecedents `hW₁`, `hW₂` at both theorem heads.

**No disjointness hypothesis on `W₁` and `W₂`.** The LN's wording is "two subsets of nodes", with no clause requiring $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$ or excluding overlap with the input set $J$. This makes the lemma a *free composition* result: hard intervention commutes with itself unconditionally, and the union `W₁ ∪ W₂` on the right-hand side handles overlap correctly. Downstream consumers — do-calculus reductions, iterated-intervention algebras — can compose hard interventions without first discharging a side condition. Adding a disjointness binder would strictly weaken the statement and is therefore omitted; sibling rows that genuinely require disjointness (because they combine hard intervention with node-splitting) introduce it locally.

**Carrier-subset transport via a dedicated helper lemma.** The nested intervention `(G.hardInterventionOn W₁ hW₁).hardInterventionOn W₂ ?_` requires a proof that `W₂ ⊆ (G.hardInterventionOn W₁ hW₁).J ∪ (G.hardInterventionOn W₁ hW₁).V`, not merely `W₂ ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V`. We expose the transport as the private helper `subset_carrier_of_hardInterventionOn` rather than inlining a `by`-block in the theorem type. Inlining was rejected because it would bloat the rendered statement on the website and duplicate the carrier-matching reasoning at every nested-intervention call site.

**CDMG equality unfolded inline as `cdmgExt`, not as a top-level extensionality lemma.** The proof opens with `have cdmgExt : ∀ {G₁ G₂ : CDMG Node}, G₁.J = G₂.J → … → G₁ = G₂`, destructured by `rintro` on both CDMGs and four `obtain rfl` substitutions. A top-level `@[ext]` lemma for `CDMG` is plausible but was not introduced here: this is the first row that needs componentwise CDMG equality, and a single-use local lemma keeps the proof self-contained. Future rows that need the same fact can promote it to a top-level lemma at that point.

**The bidirected-edge filter is two-sided, not one-sided.** This inherits the registered deviation from the underlying hard-intervention definition: an edge $(v_1, v_2) \in L$ is retained iff $v_1 \notin W$ *and* $v_2 \notin W$, rather than the LN's literal one-sided $v_2 \notin W$. The two-sided convention is forced by the symmetry axiom on $L$ — a one-sided filter would delete only one of the two ordered pairs encoding the same bidirected edge and so break symmetry. The composition step at $L$ then fuses two two-sided filters into a single two-sided filter for $W_1 \cup W_2$, landing on the same final bidirected-edge set the LN's one-sided convention would.
